skill.name: python-packaging
skill.description: Create and release Python packages using modern project structure, pyproject.toml metadata, build tooling, and PyPI publishing workflows.
skill.summary: Guide Python package structure, metadata, builds, releases, and publishing workflows.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, filesystem, and weak-crypto patterns, but most are Markdown examples rather than executable skill code. The confirmed risks are copyable publishing commands and a PyPI token file template, which are legitimate for this skill but require user caution.

medium_findings.title: PyPI Token File Template
medium_findings.description: The playbook recommends creating ~/.pypirc and shows token fields for PyPI and TestPyPI. The values are placeholders, so this is not credential theft, but users could store real publishing tokens on disk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file path and token fields are explicit, and the context is package publishing. The example uses placeholders and does not send credentials to an unknown endpoint.

medium_findings.title: Copyable Publishing and Private Index Commands
medium_findings.description: The playbook includes twine upload, TestPyPI installation, and private index commands. These are expected for packaging, but they perform network operations that can publish artifacts or use non-default package indexes.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clearly documented release workflows, not hidden execution. Risk remains because users may run them against real repositories or indexes.

low_findings.title: Markdown Syntax Misclassified as Command Execution and Weak Crypto
low_findings.description: Many static alerts come from inline code, fenced code blocks, or ordinary words such as description. I found no executable Ruby backticks or weak cryptographic algorithm usage in these locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review shows documentation syntax and normal packaging text at the flagged lines. No shell interpolation, hidden execution, or cryptographic code is present.

low_findings.title: Expected Documentation URLs
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URLs point to example project metadata, package indexes, badges, and official packaging resources. They are visible documentation references, not covert network calls.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear in project metadata examples and a resources section. I found no code that automatically requests these URLs.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential Storage Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation shows a home-directory PyPI configuration file with token fields. This is legitimate publishing guidance, but it should be treated as sensitive when adapted with real credentials.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The credential file pattern is explicitly present. The example remains lower risk because tokens are placeholders.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Publishing Workflow
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation includes commands for uploading packages and installing from alternate indexes. These operations affect external package services when a user runs them.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible and domain-appropriate, but they can publish artifacts or trust external indexes if executed.

content.value_statement: Python packaging has many standards and release steps. This skill guides project structure, metadata, builds, publishing, and verification.
actual_capabilities: Designs source-layout and flat-layout Python package structures.
Explains pyproject.toml metadata, build backends, dependencies, and entry points.
Provides examples for setuptools, hatchling, flit, poetry, and CLI packaging.
Guides wheel and source distribution builds with validation steps.
Covers TestPyPI, PyPI, private index publishing, and release checklists.
limitations: It is guidance content and does not publish packages by itself.
Users must verify package names, credentials, and repository settings before publishing.
Examples may need updates for project-specific build backends or policies.
It does not audit package source code for vulnerabilities.

use_cases.title: Package a New Library
use_cases.description: Create a clean project layout, metadata, dependency list, and build workflow for a reusable Python library.

use_cases.title: Release a CLI Tool
use_cases.description: Define console entry points, packaging metadata, tests, and release steps for an installable command-line tool.

use_cases.title: Standardize Publishing
use_cases.description: Document repeatable build, TestPyPI, PyPI, and private index publishing workflows for a team.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Package Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me package a small Python library. Recommend a project structure, required files, pyproject.toml fields, and basic validation steps.

prompt_templates.title: Add CLI Packaging
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a Python CLI project. Show how to define entry points, dependencies, test commands, and a release checklist.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a PyPI Release
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my planned Python package release. Check metadata, build steps, TestPyPI validation, token handling, and final PyPI publishing steps.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Build Backends
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare setuptools, hatchling, flit, and poetry for my package. Recommend one based on dependencies, CLI needs, versioning, and team workflow.

output_examples.input: I need to publish a small utility library for Python 3.10 and later.
output: Recommended structure using a src layout with tests and documentation.
Required metadata fields for name, version, authors, license, and dependencies.
Build and validation steps before uploading to TestPyPI.

output_examples.input: My package needs a command named report-tool after installation.
output: Entry point guidance that maps the command to the package CLI function.
Dependency and optional dependency recommendations for runtime and development use.
Verification steps for editable installs and installed command behavior.

output_examples.input: Our team uses a private package index and wants a safer release process.
output: A publishing workflow that separates build, test, upload, and install validation.
Credential handling cautions for token files and repository configuration.
A checklist for version tags, artifacts, and repository links.

best_practices: Use a src layout for packages that will be distributed or tested across environments.
Test builds locally and on TestPyPI before publishing to a production package index.
Treat PyPI and private index tokens as secrets and store them according to team policy.
anti_patterns: Publishing directly to PyPI before testing the built artifacts.
Hardcoding real tokens or repository credentials in project files or examples.
Using exact dependency pins for libraries unless there is a strong compatibility reason.

faq.question: Does this skill create package files automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance, templates, and checks that an AI assistant can apply to your project.

faq.question: Can it help with pyproject.toml?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains build-system settings, project metadata, dependencies, optional dependencies, and entry points.

faq.question: Does it support modern packaging standards?
faq.answer: Yes. It focuses on pyproject.toml, PEP 517 and PEP 518 build systems, and PEP 621 metadata.

faq.question: Can it help publish to PyPI?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers build validation, TestPyPI testing, PyPI upload steps, and release checklists.

faq.question: Is it safe to use with real tokens?
faq.answer: Use caution. The examples use placeholders, but real tokens must be stored securely and never committed.

faq.question: Does it scan package code for vulnerabilities?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on packaging structure and release workflows, not application security scanning.
